coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for vanadium(V) detection in mineral water samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lijun
Zhang, Heng
Fan, Huanhuan
Jiang, Xiuming
Zhao, Wenjie
Xiang, Guo Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we propose a simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor. We designed and developed a carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for vanadium(V) by coating the surface of dye-doped silica nanoparticles with CDs. The fluorescence of dual-emission silica nanoparticles was quenched in acetic acid through potassium bromate (KBrO3) oxidation. V(V) could catalyze KBrO3 oxidation reaction process, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence quenching of dual-emission silica nanoparticles. We investigated several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor. Under the optimized conditions, the detection limit of this nanosensor reached 1.1ngmL−1 and the linear range from 10 to 800ngmL−1. Furthermore, we found that the sensor was suitable for determination of V(V) in different mineral water samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Vanadium(V)
Silica nanoparticles
Water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
Characterization of CDs-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
Sensing Mechanism of the Ratiometric Nanosensor for Vanadium(V)
Optimization of Acid Media Type and Its Concentration
Effects of Reaction Temperature and Time on Catalytic System

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the preparation process of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles.
FT-IR spectra of RhB-doped silica nanoparticles (a), CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles (b), and CDs (c). A TEM image of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles.
The fluorescence intensity quenching trend of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles.
CDs Solution (1.0mgmL−1), KBrO3 (2.0×10−5 M) solution, Citric acid (8.0×10−5 M), concentrations of V(V) solutions (10–800ngmL−1), HAc (0.02M), Reaction temperature: 95°C, Reaction time: 7min.
Effect of acid type on the sensing system
CDs solution (1.0mgmL−1), KBrO3 (2.0×10−5 M) solution, Acid concentration (0.02M), V(V) (20ngmL−1), Citric acid (8.0×10−5 M), Reaction temperature: 95 °C, Reaction time: 7min.
Effect of the KBrO3 concentration on the sensing system.
CDs solution (1.0mgmL−1), HAc (0.02M), Citric acid (8.0×10−5 M), V(V) (20ngmL−1), Reaction temperature: 95°C, Reaction time: 7min.
Effect of reaction temperature and time on the ΔF of the system
CDs solution (1.0mgmL−1), HAc (0.02M), KBrO3 (2.0×10−5 M) solution, Citric acid (8.0×10−5 M), V(V) (20ngmL−1), Reaction temperature (70–100°C), Reaction time: (1–10min).
Determination of V(V) concentration in real samples (n =3, mean ±SD, ngmL−1).

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot-based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for vanadium(V) detection in mineral water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we propose a simple and effective strategy for designing a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor. We designed and developed a carbon dots (CDs) based dual-emission nanosensor for vanadium(V) by coating the surface of dye-doped silica nanoparticles with CDs. The fluorescence of dual-emission silica nanoparticles was quenched in acetic acid through potassium bromate (KBrO3) oxidation. V(V) could catalyze KBrO3 oxidation reaction process, resulting in the ratiometric fluorescence quenching of dual-emission silica nanoparticles. We investigated several important parameters affecting the performance of the nanosensor. Under the optimized conditions, the detection limit of this nanosensor reached 1.1ngmL−1 and the linear range from 10 to 800ngmL−1. Furthermore, we found that the sensor was suitable for determination of V(V) in different mineral water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanadium, an essential element for animals and humans, is released into the environment by burning fossil and from various industry processes. Much attention has been focused on biological roles of vanadium, its toxicity in living organisms, and extent of environmental pollution [1–3].

Different analytical methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [4,5], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [6], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7] and spectrophotometry [8–11], have been most frequently used for the determination of vanadium compounds. However, these methods have been scarcely applied for the direct determination of vanadium in samples with complicated matrices. Moreover, these are not suitable for routine analysis because of high-cost instrumentation. In this context, kinetic methods based on catalyzed reactions are well known for their simplicity, sensitivity, selectivity, and low cost of instrumentation [12–14]. These methods are based on catalytic properties of vanadium concerning the oxidation of certain organic compounds [15–20]. Recently, quantum dots (QDs) have been reported to act as fluorescent probe for vanadium based on the fluorescence quenching effect of V(V) on QDs [21].

Owing to their superior fluorescent properties, easy preparation, and low cost, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit great potential for fluorescence-based analytical applications [22,23]. CDs have been extensively investigated as fluorophores for sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Cu2+) [24–27], anions (e.g., NO2 −) [28], and other organic compounds (e.g., glucose glyphosate) [29–31].

Here we present a ratiometric fluorescence probe prepared through grafting CDs and rhodamine (RhB) on silica nanoparticles. Vanadium-catalyzed fluorometry was optimized for the determination of V(V) based on the enhancement of quenching effect attributed to catalytic oxidation reactions of fluorophores on nanoparticles in presence of V(V). In addition, the developed analytical method was successfully applied for the determination of V(V) in mineral water samples with satisfactory results.

Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer was used for recording emission spectra. Using a JEM-2011 TEM, transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were captured. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in KBr were collected on a WQF-510 FT-IR spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Co, Ltd., Beijing, China). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2450 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation).

N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS), aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), rhodamine B (RhB), and anhydrous citric acid (AR) were purchased from Aladdin (www.aladdin-e.com, China). Tetraethoxysilane (AR) and potassium bromate (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Ammonium metavanadate (AR) was purchased from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Ethanol, anhydrous citric acid, NH4OH, HAc and other reagents were purchased from Beijing Chemical, China. Ultrapure water (18MΩcm−1) was used in all the experiments.

The CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared following a reported method [24,25], and was also reported in our previous publication [32]. The preparation procedure included three main steps, as described in Fig.1 . The organosilane functionalized CDs were first prepared by the pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid in AEAPMS at 230°C for 1min [33]. After the purification using petroleum ether, the obtained product (0.4mL) was added to 1.6mL ethanol.

In this step, RhB was pre-coupled with APTES through the reaction between carboxylic group of rhodamine and amino group of APTES in ethanol under constant stirring for 24h at room temperature. The obtained RhB-APAES solution (2mL), TEOS (500μL) and ammonia (1mL) were added to ethanol (40mL), and the mixture was left under constant stirring at room temperature for 8h. Then, TEOS (800μL) was added, and the reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the silica nanoparticles were collected and washed three times with ethanol. Next, the obtained nanoparticles were ultrasonically dispersed in 40mL ethanol for 20min. Then, TEOS (100μL) and ammonia (500μL) were added to the solution, and the mixture was stirred for 24h at room temperature. The formed dye-doped silica nanoparticles were finally collected by centrifugation and washed three times with ethanol, followed by redispersion in 40mL ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Dye-doped silica nanoparticles (40mL) obtained above was mixed with ammonia (500μL). After ultrasonicating and stirring for 10min each, a 100μL volume CDs was added to the mixture and stirred for 15h at room temperature. The reaction mixture was centrifuged, and the obtained CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were washed three times with ethanol before redispersing in ethanol at a concentration of 10mgmL−1.

First, a 1.0mL solution of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles (10mgmL−1) was taken in a 10mL graduated tube containing 1.0mL HAc solution (2.0M), 0.5mL KBrO3 solution (4.0×10−4 M), and 1.0mL citric acid solution (8.0×10−4 M). A certain amount of the vanadium(V) standard solution (or the sample solution) was added to the test tube. After diluting with ultrapure water to the required volume, the mixture was shaken thoroughly and then left to rest for 7min in a water bath at 95°C. The test tubes were cooled down to room temperature holding under running cold water, and the fluorescence of the test solutions were then recorded. Simultaneously, a blank sample was prepared without addition of the vanadium(V) standard solution or sample solution.

The fluorescence intensities of the dual emission peaks (470nm and 580nm) for the test solution (F470 and F580) and blank (F0 470 and F0 580) were directly measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation at 360nm. The spectrometric quantities were calculated according to: ΔF470 =F0 470 −F470, ΔF580 =F0 580 −F580, and ΔF470 /ΔF580.
Different drinking water samples were collected from the local supermarket (Zhengzhou, China), and the water samples were analyzed without further treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs-Coated Dual-Emission Silica Nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles were prepared by the reaction between amine and carboxyl groups. The formation of amide linkage was confirmed by recording the FT-IR spectra of the product. Fig. 2c shows typical signals of CONR vibration at 1654cm−1 (CO) and 3456cm−1 (NH). The peaks at 800cm−1 and 1100cm−1 can be attributed to the SiO and SiCH2 stretching vibrations. These data clearly demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles. In addition, Fig. 2 illustrates a TEM image of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles and reveals an average diameter of ~100nm for the CD coated nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Mechanism of the Ratiometric Nanosensor for Vanadium(V)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 illustrates the fluorescence spectra of the CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles -CA-HAc-KBrO3-V(V) systems. As evident from the Fig. 3, the CDs-coated silica nanoparticles exhibit two emission peaks (470nm and 580nm) when excited at the wavelength of 360nm. We assigned these two peaks to emissions induced by CDs (470nm) and RhB (580nm). Fig. 3 also depicts a slight quenching in the fluorescence signals of both the emission peaks initiated by oxidation of KBrO3 in acidic media. However, we observed significant enhancements in the fluorescence quenching effect when V(V) was added to the system in presence of a certain amount of citric acid. Furthermore, higher the V(V) concentration, greater was the fluorescence quenching effect. This could be attributed to the catalytic activity of V(V) for the oxidation reaction between KBrO3 and CDs-coated silica nanoparticles. In other words, the oxidation of the dual-emission fluorescent nanoparticles by KBrO3 was accelerated in presence of V(V). The degree of acceleration (ΔF470 /ΔF580) showed a linear relationship with V(V) concentration.

To investigate the effects of different conditions, such as acid type and concentration, the concentration of citric acid, the concentration of KBrO3, the reaction temperature and time, on the fluorescence quenching effect of the system containing 20ngmL−1 V(V), we followed a univariate approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Acid Media Type and Its Concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The type of acidic medium is an important factor for the oxidation process, where KBrO3 is used as strong oxidant. To examine the effect of acid type on the ΔF of the system, we used different acids such as HCl, H2SO4, HAc, and H3PO4. Fig. 4 clearly shows that ΔF is much larger in HAc than that in HCl, H2SO4, and H3PO4. This could be explained by the fact that KBrO3 showed a strong oxidative property in strong acid media, which resulting in a fast oxidation process even without catalyst. And in weak acid media, the catalytic effect of V(V) was obviously showed. Hence, we selected HAc as the reaction medium.

Furthermore, we optimized the concentration of HAc. The results presented in Fig.S1 show a maximum yield of ΔF470 at 0.02M HAc, and the ΔF580 changed slightly within the testing HAc concentration range. Therefore, we selected 0.02M HAc as the reaction medium.

According to the sensing mechanism, the concentration of oxidant (KBrO3) affects the ΔF of the catalyzing system. Hence, KBrO3 determines the sensitivity of the method based on the catalyzing system. We studied the effect of KBrO3 in a concentration range of 1.0×10−6–5.0×10−5 M. We found that both ΔF470 and ΔF580 of the catalyzing system increased as the concentration of KBrO3 increased from 5.0×10−6 to 2.0×10−5 M and then decreased with further increase in the concentration of KBrO3 (Fig. 5 ). The results indicated that moderate concentration of KBrO3 makes a clear catalytic effect of V(V), and the catalytic effect decreased in higher oxidation reaction process with higher concentration of KBrO3. Based on this finding, we selected 2.0×10−5 M KBrO3 as the optimum oxidant concentration.

The use of proper activators for catalytic reactions offers an improvement in both sensitivity and selectivity [14]. There are many reports on vanadium catalyzed indicator reactions where some ligands acted as activators [34–38]. In the vanadium catalyzed azine dyes-bromate reactions, citric acid acted as a good activator, enhancing selectivity and increasing sensitivity [39]. In this work, we also investigated the activating effect of citric acid for the CDs-coated silica nanoparticles-bromate system. Fig. S2 shows the activating effect of citric acid for the catalytic system. From the results in Fig. S2, we found that the activating effect of citric acid was confirmed by the fact that both ΔF470 and ΔF580 were greatly increased with the presence of citric acid in the catalytic system. Fig. S3 shows the effect of citric acid concentration on the ΔF in the concentration range of 2.0×10−5 to 1.2×10−4 M. We observed a pronounced increase in ΔF (both ΔF470 and ΔF580) with citric acid concentration up to 8.0×10−5 M, but ΔF gradually decreased when citric acid concentration beyond 8.0×10−5 M. As a ligand activator, a certain concentration is necessary for the effect activation; however, citric acid as a reductant, it could also affect the oxidation process with higher concentrations. Hence, we selected 8.0×10−5 M as the optimum activator (citric acid) concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Reaction Temperature and Time on Catalytic System
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we investigated the effects of reaction temperature and time on the catalyzing system. As shown in Fig. 6 , we found a similar trend for ΔF470 and ΔF580 when the reaction temperature was increased from 70°C to 95°C. Both ΔF470 and ΔF580 increased as the temperature rose up to 95°C, followed by a significant decrease at higher temperatures. This was probably induced by the catalysis effect, which was more efficient at higher temperatures (95°C). On the other hand, the bare fluorescence intensities of CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles sharply declined at temperatures higher than 95°C, which, in turn, reduced both ΔF470 and ΔF580. Therefore, the reaction temperature was optimized to 95°C.

While studying for the optimized reaction time at the optimal reaction temperature according, we found that 7min was sufficient for both ΔF470 and ΔF580 to reach maxima, and ΔF greatly declined for longer reaction periods. Based on this finding, we selected 7min as the optimized reaction time and used for further studies.

To examine the selectivity, we studied the effects of potential interferences from some metal ions, as well as other oxidants and reductants on the V(V) sensing system. The solutions containing V(V) (20ngmL−1) and selected interferences were treated according to recommended procedures under the optimum conditions. Table 1 lists the tolerance limit of the coexisting materials, defined as the maximum concentration to achieve <10% variation in fluorescence intensities of both emission peaks. We found that most metal ions and anions exhibited high tolerance limits.

Under the optimum conditions, the concentration of V(V) showed a linear trend as a function of ΔF470/ΔF580 in the range of 10–800ngmL−1. The detection limit (3σ) of this method, determined according to IUPAC, was 1.1ngmL−1 for V(V), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 4.0% (c=30ngmL−1, n =11).

In real samples analysis, we first constructed a standard calibration curve and then applied the proposed method to determine V(V) levels in different brands of mineral water. Table 2 summarizes the analytical data and recovery values of three types of mineral water samples and shows the recovery values between 91.0% and 104.2%.

This study describes a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor developed based on the catalytic effect of KBrO3 oxidation on CDs-coated dual-emission silica nanoparticles for V(V) detection. The catalyzed sensing system exhibited many advantages including rapid detection, good selectivity, high sensitivity, and a wide linear response range. Importantly, the study findings suggest the potential of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor for V(V) detection in mineral water samples.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21205028), Program for Science and Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (Grant No. 13HASTIT016e), and Foundation for Young Core Teachers teacher of Henan University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2017.08.010.
